author: @TheNewVaultOfKnowledge
text: Do you know if the newer multi head printers can do these temp adjustments between layers of different filaments as they do color swaps to other spools and heads of differing material, say tpu to pla and back for more ability than just a a sheet type simple sliced file? I'm experimenting with different filaments and temperatures for differing rigidity vs soft and durable in prints and the slicing seems to be very precise for what you're looking for in the print.

author: @ThatRobHuman
text: the channel Functional Print Friday just recently put out a video about printing bellows in TPU wherein he uses a flat geometry where the walls of the bellows are only one or two layers thick and an interface layer of PLA (or PETG). I think he needs to think in terms of origami to get tubular bellows, but he's on a good track as I don't think bellows printed his way will kink - you might want to have a look.

author: @MemphisMechanic

author: @MrFujack103
text: I use PVA and water then wipe a mirror over with a sponge and when dry it’s like a frosted window. It’s great and you can get the mixture just how you like it. I get mirror finishes off the bottom of my prints

author: @generation-x406
text: Try a salt solution that dries, curious if the rough crystal structure would work. Naturally non toxic, dissolvable, cheap.

author: @RahereTheGenius
text: From rigidity theory in ropework, work-hardening occurs 4/3 of the thickness away from the point at which a rope stiffens in the knot. You may do better producing the shell in the softer material and applying the harder one as a buttress to its surface, making sure no shear planes are created.

author: @the_natrix9959
text:
Aqua net, purple Can!!

Generic stuff Purple can! As long as it comes close to Aquanet primary ingredient.

Apply it to the bed. Place bed on heat tray. Warm to 65c. You will see the liquid dry. Hit print.

author: @collie147
text: Hairspray is an old 3d printing hack for glass beds.  It does eventually gunk up stuff (don't spray it in your printer because it will gunk up the machine) but it dissolves readily in soapy water.  Just haven't had cause to use it since PEI and textured beds have been around.  Nice suggestion on increasing the temp on PLA to get it to bond to TPU.

author: @piconano
text:
I used to have a lot of problems with parts not sticking, until I started cleaning the print bed with 99% EPA.
The z-axis set at -2.35 mm for the first layer at 30mm/sec print speed, makes perfect prints every time. 
Yes it's slow for the first layer, but you'll save BIG in the long run. 
The oils on your hand and environment makes it not stick. The distance of nozzle from the bed and speed of first layer is most critical in bed adhesion.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: thanks for the tips mate - cheers

author: @robrs210
text:
Never use tpu on a pei sheet always use the power coated sheets and as you have used,hairspray. There is an issue though. Only use the hairsprays with polyvinyl in the ingredients. 
The only downside is the buildup on the sheet after a lot of prints. But hot water wash and it’s all off. No mess like glue stick

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate

author: @imonseii
text: worth noting about hair spray: you want the kind that has some form of vinyl in it. vinyl acetate (which is the VA in PVA) is what makes some kinds of hair spray excellent for 3d print adhesion, and when the hairspray lacks this ingredient, is absolutely awful for our needs.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: thanks for the tip mate

author: @kimtyson9101
text: I am planning to use TPU on my new printer XMAX3 with the flexible PEI sheet but am a bit wary now since they run at $60+ US.  I used to print TPU on my MakerBot TOM and Prusa i3V with blue tape and kapton tape without issue.  So has anyone tried heating the back of the flexible build plate with a hair dryer to get the TPU off - IE no glue?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I read folks had done that mate and reported good results - i have printed small TPU parts and had no problems it was only with the large sheet the sticking issue came up

author: @justtinkering6713

author: @lv_woodturner3899
text:
Nice tip for PLA and TPU.
So far I have only printed PLA.  My PEI sheet is still working after 14 months as long as I clean the surface with acetone after each print.   If I clean with alcohol, the print is likely to come off the sheet.    I heard about hairspray but have not tried.   I tried glue stick, it works, but a hassle to clean off.    With my previous printer, I used the blue masking tape.   I prefer my present printers PEI sheet, no hassle - as long as I clean it.
Dave.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I am with you on that mate

author: @lagunafishing
text:
Those prints come out clean eh?
I've seen people talk about using pritstik and hairspray for a long time but never had the need printing on glass as I do. Interesting method of fusing two different materials together by increasing the temperature on the first layer on the top coat. I already have my code set for higher temp for first layer anyway to ensure good adhesion to the bed and tweaking it is pretty much straight forward. Good info Rob!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I am glad you found it interesting mate - cheers

author: @stuartlark6441
text: Hi Rob, I've used, glue sticks, hairspray and painters tape with varying degrees of success but the best thing I came across that gives near 100 percent results is something called Magigoo. It cost quite a bit more than the others but you put it on so thinly you actually use very little of it and one coat lasts for print after print and if you let the build plate get cold the print practically falls off the plate but when the plate is up to temperature the object is locked on there. Try it out, I think you'll be pleasantly surprised. Cheers

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I will do mate - that's great advice thanks for sharing

replies.author: @petersilie3090
replies.text: That's exactly how my experiments went. For me, the solution was 3D-LAC.  I use it on a simple 3mm glass plate (old window pane) and it works excellently.

replies.author: @Adrian_Galilea
replies.text:
Glass cleaner!

100% reliable
Cheapest
Mess-free

I've tried everything and y'all should try this ;)

You just have to apply a bit directly on the plate and then spread it out with a clean lint-free fabric, I use paper towel.

I've got a bottle since I got my X1C and there's more than 50% left, after so many spools of filament.

author: @Alkimi
text: Hair spray is great but I found that blue painter's masking tape is even better, although sometimes the bond is to strong abdomen I have to sand it off the print afterwards. The great thing about using masking tape is that it will hold the print in place even if the bottom layer warps and tries to pop off of the bed.

text: i did try the other day a coloured laser print and 3d printing on top, unfortunately printing at 210 degrees (pla) didnt really get the paper hot enough to transfer the laser toner over and all i got was a hint of the colour, I guess I will need to try with a hotter printing material such as ABS

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

replies.author: @scotttovey
replies.text:
I looked up something that took me to an article on making PCBs with a laser printer.
In one of the articles, the individual mentioned using the backing paper that comes with peel and stick labels to transfer the printed image to the PCB.
I'm not sure that filament will stick to the paper label paper though.
If it will stick on the peel and stick paper, it may also stick to wax paper.
Otherwise, one may be able to transfer an image to the printed part using an iron, in the same way that it is transfered to PCBs.

replies.author: @the_natrix9959
replies.text:
Correct, extra hold is important. If it won't hold your banges straight up in 20mph winds, it won't work on your bed.😂

I have been using Max strength hairspray for years. I used it on borosilicate beds and no longer had to mess around with first layer slicer adjustments. Never looked back. The internet seems to be very proud and anecdotal. That mixes with engineering as water and oil(p.s. of course, these don't mix, internet)

I still laugh at glue sticks. Hahaha, have at it. Dimensional instability is a great way to start a print. No way, you have a perfect print with an uneven bed.

author: @petersilie3090
text: Better than hairspray, also for PETG and PLA, is 3D-Lac (ask google). If the adhesion with TPU is too great, a little isopropanol on the heating plate helps.

replies.author: @petersilie3090
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering My attempts with hairspray have not worked, although others have recommended a certain product.  One can of 3D-Lac is enough for a lot
 of filament spools. The cost of the 3D-Lac is no longer an issue.

author: @VoidySA

author: @poepflater
text: Honestly TPU is the MOST forgiving filament for attaching to the buildplate, you probably don't even need to do hairspray (which I do apply from time to time with PLA). It is my favourite for useful prints...but my plate is glass
